speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Rosner, when did you first hear about the film Steven Spielberg was going to make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had been in contact with Paul Page, Poldek Pfefferberg. And I knew and I read in papers that they had started the film in Poland. It was in black and white. And one day, I got a letter from Oskar-- from Steven Spielberg inviting us to Israel for epilogue of the film, which was make-- in colors, in Israel-- quite a long letter and all expenses paid. And of course, we went to Israel. We met Steven Spielberg and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ben Kingsley and Liam Neeson and a lot of people which I haven't seen for 50 years used to be together in concentration camps. A lot have died, of course. A lot of-- I didn't recognize. He make it a big party in King David Hotel. He engage four musicians from Poland-- Poles, not Jews playing Jewish music-- very good, very good. And then I was also playing a little bit for Liam Neeson. I play some Irish tunes. And I play for Ben Kingsley and for, for Spielberg. Then he wrote to me, Leo, you are an artist. I got it here-- Spielberg. Anyhow, it was very moving, this filming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the film was released in '83. We've been invited eight times for the premiers, six time in Melbourne, in Canberra, in Sydney, then again in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it feel like seeing yourself on the screen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not, you know-- I am rather hard-skinned. But, but Helene is very-- see, I, I-- I've been also-- I show-- before the-- all of them I show special the whole film for us in my family. And my grandchildren and children were with us and-- with a lot of crying. And of course my wife was crying. I was also wet eyes. And my grandchildren say, Papa, we are with you. Don't worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you any more questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're asking all-- all the time asking-- ask questions. But they know everything-- what I know, they-- everything. I had lot of-- I've been going to a lot of schools, all different denominations and churches, and the Melbourne Uni, Monash Uni, had quite a lot of talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've been in one Catholic girls' school, very religious. And at the end, after talking, I had my accordion. I said, any requests? So one girl asked, 'Hava Nagila.'

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] And I sang it. Some language-- I don't know, was Latin [LAUGHS] I'm singing it, you know, not Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find a lot of questions from non-Jewish audiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Most popular question is about Schindler-- why he did it?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I couldn't care less. I wish more people like him would be-- more people would be saved. More people would be alived. What-- the reason, you can't go in somebody's brain. What-- he wasn't-- he, he was very rich on the beginning of the war. And he died very poor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's true-- is he-- after the war, as I said, he got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this farm in Argentina with his wife. He left his wife. He went back to Germany to Frankfurt, where he continued to drink-- and womanizing. And he'd been going from there quite often to Israel to see the survivors. And they welcomed him like a king each time. And in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '76, I think, '74, he died in Germany. His liver gave up from drinking, you know. And his wish was to be buried in Israel. But they brought his body to Israel. And I have seen on video, on television, his funeral in Catholic church. Apart from his body and him, whole church was full with Jews with covered heads in church. And he was buried in Mount Olive in Israel, Catholic cemetery. And this is where we've been doing this epilogue filming, been walking slowly towards the stone, stone, and putting pebbles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also people ask us, what's the meaning of pebbles? I said, the religious Jews don't put flowers on the stump-- on the tomb, only-- any pebbles for memory that they've been there. That's the custom. Would you describe yourself as a religious man now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am-- I am just traditional.

speaker: interviewer
text: But has your faith changed since, since through-- since the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. See, one-- I, I-- in Poland, I didn't speak Yiddish. And I learned Yiddish here in Australia. I speak Yiddish. I speak German, French, and English and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a musical family still? Are you a musical family? Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. My all grandchildren are very, very musical, very musical. And my youngest grandson is studying music in Lismore. He got a diploma. He want to get degree. He want to be music teacher. My second son is a very good drummer. He got diploma in landscaping engineering and works in a very big, American firm. And the third works in Triple R radio, married, and also plays guitar, very musical. And the granddaughters are-- all three in Mount Scopus College. They are very musical, got very lovely voice. And when, when Frances, my youngest daughter-- my younger daughter was three years old, I bought her little cymbals-- glockenspiel, you know. That's from C to C, the white notes, not black notes. And I start to play 'Yankee Doodle.' [SINGING] Da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da-- should be B-flat. But there wasn't. And I played, [SINGING] Da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da. She said, Daddy, that note is not from here. That's a good ear She was three years old, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she got perfect pitch now. This is one-- I got also perfect pitch. This is one in millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Is there anything about Europe that you miss or have missed when you first came--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I've been--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: --since about five years ago back in Poland. I was very disappointed. It shrunk. After-- Australia is such a big country, and Poland is so small. When you drive a car 200 meters, it's already different village. And here, you can drive and drive and drive and drive-- it's nothing at the distances. And I-- my-- got still in Cracow my niece. Yeah, [? Anisha Horovitz ?] is the one daughter who was in the film who give a cake to Oskar Schindler. He kissed her. She lives there in Cracow. She married out-- married a doctor. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my other nephew Richard Horovitz is a very famous photographer. He lives in America. He's going quite often to Poland. I have a talk and show of his films. He's a very unusual photographer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Rosner, in conclusion, why would you say that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only thanks to music, definitely. Music saved my life. See, if I wouldn't be a musician, I wouldn't go-- wouldn't survive definitely. First, I was on the list because Schindler likes our music. And even Amon Goeth, once he called us and told us to cut hair-- cut the hair, come back, to shave off the eyebrow and mustache-- shave all over. And he sent us and the guard to Cracow to find two musicians-- one accordionist with long nose and mustache, one violinist with long nose and mustache-- to have alibi in case we find out that somebody-- once, he took us out of the concentration camp to entertain some German party, which wasn't allowed. Somebody dubbed him in. And he want to make us unrecognizable. See, he, he could just shoot us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he saved you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he ask us to cut off eyebrow and, and mustache. It didn't come to this. But to-- see, so you-- it's unpredictable. Once, at lunchtime with his girlfriend, she caught a fly. He said, how could you be so barbaric? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was a court case in Cracow. They caught him after the war and hang him in Cracow. Well, the court-- I got a book in Polish from the court case. He want to call on us that he was good to us. See, each time I was going there, I wasn't sure that I come back. My wife didn't know if I come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Rosner, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was pleasure, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, Leo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Chaim or Henry Rosner. He played violin also. We played together from childhood. Actually, he started to teach me violin. But I wasn't very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry? What year? Oh, only guess-- about 1932 or 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow. This is my mother, Freidel or [? Francesca ?] [NON-ENGLISH] Rosner. She had nine children. A lot of work-- a very hard-working woman, little woman, but lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken maybe in some '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grunberg. And when was this-- where was this photograph taken? Probably when I was very little. Probably in Cracow, not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photograph, Leo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Cracow Salon Orchestra under the direction of my brother Henry. He was very young then there. He was about 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is the middle. In center is he, standing. And next to him, on the left side, is my father with the violin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seated, with, with the bow tie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, always bow tie, yeah, here. And I don't remember the name of the others, but was about 10 of them in the orchestra.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when with this photograph have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about 1933, 1934 in Cracow. This is myself when I was young, about in 1936. This photograph was taken in Cracow. I was wearing tile suit with white bow tie. This is my sister Mela, Melanie, with her husband. That is in about 1936, 1937. She played beautiful accordion. She was married. And she had a little boy, one half-- two and a half. And German grabbed the child and killed-- in my eyes, killed the child, hit against the wall. And they took her away. I never seen her again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Simon-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Zanger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the little boy's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Uszu. ?] This is in Munich. I play with Henry on stage. And in 1935, 1936--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, I beg your pardon, '46 in Munich. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do, do you remember the name where you played-- of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Pont ?] [? Vivre-- ?] there was a theater. And the other-- and that one is in London in 1983 when we play in interview for television, Thames Television in London. That one is my sister, Regina, with [? Herman, ?] Henry, in New York. That was in about 1983, not long ago. After, she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute-- is your sister-in-law again-- Can you tell me about this picture, Leo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, starting from the right side, I am sitting with Helene. Then standing in the middle with glasses is Poldek Pfefferberg, Paul Page. In front of him is his wife with glasses. Next to him is my sister-in-law, Janka-- Jane [? Gross, ?] Janka [? Gross ?] and Joe [? Gross. ?] And in the center is Thomas Keneally.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's seated. And who is standing behind Thomas Keneally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some friends. Quite a few are not alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the occasion of this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after the book was written. We make a party, a get-together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In here, in this room. See, on background, you can see the same picture, this sail here on the wall. This is last month with my wife on the celebration of my youngest granddaughter bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a big occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: A month ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last, last month, yes, last month. This is my daughter Anna and Frances singing on some occasion about two years ago. And I am playing-- accompanying them on accordion. This is couple years ago, my three grandsons. In front is Ben, then Paul, and in the back, Danny-- beautiful boys and very musical. That-- also last year, my three granddaughters-- and [? Gabi, ?] the oldest one. In the center is [? Jessie, ?] the youngest one. And on the right side is Carly. We celebrate birthday on the same date but different year. [LAUGHS] Well, this is the famous or infamous tune which I play with my brother Henry for the camp commandant where one of the officers asked us to play the tune, 'Gloomy Sunday.' And he commit suicide. [SINGING - 'GLOOMY SUNDAY'] That's all.
